comment_text.sn22.8:1.5: This directly rejects all forms of self, especially the conceptions of self as expressed in the Upaniṣads. | Etaṁ mama occurs as mamedaṁ (“this is mine”, Maitrī Upaniṣad 3.2), which in context is a limited view of self. An illegitimate king betrays his country with the thought, “It is not mine” (na etan mama, Arthaśāstra 8.2.08). | Eso’hamasmi is Yājñavalkya’s so’hamasmi (“I am that”, Bṛhadāraṇyaka Upaniṣad 1.4.1; BU 5.15.1 = Īśā Upaniṣad 16; Chāndogya Upaniṣad 4.11.1, 4.12.1, 4.13.1; Śāṅkhāyana Āraṇyaka 3.6), which Uddālaka’s tattvamasi puts in second person (Chāndogya Upaniṣad 6.8.7). | The same passage also parallels eso me attā with sa ātmā (“that is the self”), for which we also find eṣa ma ātmā (CU 3.14.3), and in the optative mood, ma idaṁ syād ātman (BU 1.2.7).

root_text.sn22.8:1.5: Idha, bhikkhave, assutavā puthujjano rūpaṁ ‘etaṁ mama, esohamasmi, eso me attā’ti samanupassati.
root_text.sn22.8:1.6: Tassa taṁ rūpaṁ vipariṇamati aññathā hoti.
root_text.sn22.8:1.11: viññāṇaṁ ‘etaṁ mama, esohamasmi, eso me attā’ti samanupassati.
root_text.sn22.8:1.12: Tassa taṁ viññāṇaṁ vipariṇamati aññathā hoti.
root_text.sn22.8:2.2: Idha, bhikkhave, sutavā ariyasāvako rūpaṁ ‘netaṁ mama, nesohamasmi, na meso attā’ti samanupassati.
root_text.sn22.8:2.3: Tassa taṁ rūpaṁ vipariṇamati aññathā hoti.
root_text.sn22.8:2.8: viññāṇaṁ ‘netaṁ mama, nesohamasmi, na meso attā’ti samanupassati.
root_text.sn22.8:2.9: Tassa taṁ viññāṇaṁ vipariṇamati aññathā hoti.

translation_text.sn22.8:1.2: “Mendicants, I will teach you how grasping leads to anxiety, and how not grasping leads to freedom from anxiety.
translation_text.sn22.8:1.3: Listen and apply your mind well, I will speak.
translation_text.sn22.8:1.4: And how does grasping lead to anxiety?
translation_text.sn22.8:1.5: It’s when an unlearned ordinary person regards form like this: ‘This is mine, I am this, this is my self.’
translation_text.sn22.8:1.6: But that form of theirs decays and perishes,
translation_text.sn22.8:1.7: which gives rise to sorrow, lamentation, pain, sadness, and distress.
translation_text.sn22.8:1.11: consciousness like this: ‘This is mine, I am this, this is my self.’
translation_text.sn22.8:1.12: But that consciousness of theirs decays and perishes,
translation_text.sn22.8:1.13: which gives rise to sorrow, lamentation, pain, sadness, and distress.
translation_text.sn22.8:1.14: That’s how grasping leads to anxiety.
translation_text.sn22.8:2.1: And how does not grasping lead to freedom from anxiety?
translation_text.sn22.8:2.2: It’s when a learned noble disciple regards form like this: ‘This is not mine, I am not this, this is not my self.’
translation_text.sn22.8:2.3: When that form of theirs decays and perishes,
translation_text.sn22.8:2.4: it doesn’t give rise to sorrow, lamentation, pain, sadness, and distress.
translation_text.sn22.8:2.8: consciousness like this: ‘This is not mine, I am not this, this is not my self.’
translation_text.sn22.8:2.9: When that consciousness of theirs decays and perishes,
translation_text.sn22.8:2.10: it doesn’t give rise to sorrow, lamentation, pain, sadness, and distress.
translation_text.sn22.8:2.11: That’s how not grasping leads to freedom from anxiety.”
